[166:17]that these special operations, the covert operations, the pressure, these things will

[166:22]form sort of a pincer, and the Maduro regime will not survive the next four years.

[166:29]There's another piece of the puzzle too, which is that Venezuela's neighbor, Bolivia, which

[166:34]was run by socialists for 20 years, just elected a conservative president.

[167:05]And, as I said before, so this is a situation there, I want to take this together with what's

[167:11]happening elsewhere.

[167:12]As I said earlier, this is part of a broader strategy.

[167:16]The biggest development in Latin America is the intrusion of China into what is generally

[167:23]considered for the past 200 years, America's rightful sphere of influence.

[167:28]since President James Monroe,

[167:31]which says that we're not going to interfere

[167:33]in the great power politics of Europe.

[167:36]We're not necessarily going to interfere in a major way

[167:39]in the politics of Eurasia, unless forced to,

[167:44]like we were when we were attacked in Pearl Harbor

[167:47]or in other situations throughout the Cold War.

[167:50]But for 200 years, it's very clear

[167:52]that the Western hemisphere is our domain.

[167:54]And why is that? It's very simple.

[167:56]The United States is insulated from threats from other strategic competitors like Russia, China,

[168:03]or in another time, France, Germany, or the United Kingdom by the oceans.

[168:07]It's not feasible for any country, any major dominant country on the Eurasian land mass

[168:13]to threaten us, because in order to even get to us, they have to cross a major ocean. They have

[168:22]And so the United States has this splendid isolation.

[168:26]This is, this is 101.

[168:29]We don't have to worry as much about Europe or Asia.

[168:32]We don't have to suppress or manage great power politics

[168:37]because in order for them to threaten us,

[168:40]they would have to land an army in California

[168:42]or the Eastern Seaboard.

[168:43]And that's just not gonna happen.

[168:46]That being said,

[168:47]For the United States to remain safe, we have to govern everything that does border the United States, which is North America and South America.

[168:57]Maybe France can't threaten us, Russia can't threaten us directly, China can't threaten us directly, or Japan for that matter.

[169:06]They can't threaten the homeland directly.

[169:09]But if they gained a foothold in Central or South America, then they could.

[169:17]On the Pacific coast of South America, in Peru and Ecuador and Chile, in Panama and

[169:23]Central America, maybe then they could threaten the United States.

[169:27]That's why it's incumbent on us to keep all of that influence out.

[169:31]And that influence comes in many different forms.

[169:33]It doesn't start with a country pledging fealty to China.

[169:37]It starts with trade relations.

[169:42]And what has happened from 2015 to 2025

[169:45]is that Central and South America increasingly

[169:49]are conducting more trade with China.

[169:52]And in most of those countries,

[169:54]China is now their largest trading partner.

[169:56]And that makes them dependent on China.

[169:59]And China starts to come in with credit.

[170:01]They start to loan these countries money.

[170:03]And they loan these countries money

[170:05]to build up critical infrastructure,

[170:07]all kinds of things, radar dishes, ports.

[170:11]communication, transportation, infrastructure,

[170:13]China provides the investment.

[170:15]And in exchange, these countries give China control

[170:18]over the infrastructure.

[170:19]They call this dual use infrastructure.

[170:23]And so for example, in Argentina,

[170:25]China is building giant dishes,

[170:27]which could be used for a commercial application,

[170:29]could also be used for a military application in the future.

[170:33]And if China is providing the credit and Argentina gives them control over it, let's say they

[170:38]get a 20 year lease on what they build.

[170:41]Well, it's foreseeable that in 20 years, maybe China will use that against the United States

[170:47]in some strategic capacity.

[170:49]And that goes for all the infrastructure they're building.

[170:52]This is unacceptable to the United States.

[170:56]This has happened slowly over 10 years the trade relation intensifies China builds infrastructure governments are elected that have a left-leaning ideology and

[171:07]So they're ideologically aligned with Beijing which is technically a communist country

[171:11]And so these historically left-wing or socialist governments

[171:16]Patronist governments like in Argentina or Bolivia Venezuela Peru

[171:40]and get replacement parts for the weapons,

[171:43]they need to be dependent on China's arms industry.

[171:50]This is how a country like China,

[171:53]a strategic competitor could come to threaten

[172:02]And so this is why across the board,

[172:04]the United States is engaging in an all out offense

[172:07]on all these countries.

[186:47]I think that it is actually better

[186:49]for Americans to have an empire.

[186:51]I think we want to have an empire.

[186:54]That's always been my position on this.

[186:57]And to the extent that Israel is

[187:01]a hostile power to the extent that Israel is going against us,

[187:05]then maybe they don't fit into that equation,

[187:06]but the Western Hemisphere is ours.

[187:49]We want the energy.

[188:57]wealth and power, that's not America first.

[189:28]They're securing their periphery.

[189:54]just think we should be more honest about them. And I think we should be more strategic about them.

[190:21]But I am saying that to dismiss that

[190:24]Automatically and say that you know, that's not America first. I think we need to start to think a little bit beyond

[190:31]That kind of small-minded version of America first because to tell you the truth

[190:37]Americans do benefit actually

[190:40]From being the the foremost superpower in the world we benefit from primacy

[190:48]It's good for America to have as much power as possible. I don't know about you guys. Maybe

[190:52]this is just my personal philosophy and my political philosophy. I think that it's good

[190:58]for America to have as much power as possible because the more power that we have, the more

[191:05]that we can use it to secure our interests, which is our own safety, our own prosperity,

[191:12]That's good, actually.

[191:14]And to the extent that America has made mistakes, it is because we have overcommitted in ways

[191:19]that don't actually enhance the position of America and Americans.

[191:26]Overturning Saddam Hussein is something that didn't really have a ton of benefits for Americans

[191:30]in our position in the world.

[191:32]It may have actually hurt us, but it did benefit Israel.

[191:35]That was the problem there.

[191:42]And everything that I believe in for America first is strengthening our position.

[191:47]We want to be the dominant force on the globe.

[191:51]We don't want the Jews telling us what to do.

[191:54]We don't want China telling us what to do.

[191:57]We don't want any country any people any religion to stand in our way. That's the goal

[192:02]We want to free ourselves from the influence of the Jews and immigrants at home

[192:06]We want to free ourselves from the pressure and the threat of our strategic competitors abroad

[192:11]Then that starts with turning the Western hemisphere into a fortress. I'm a believer in that. That's what autarky is about

[192:18]That's what tariffs are about

[192:21]This is about American revanchism we want to rebuild America

[192:26]That's what making America great again is about.

[192:29]What does greatness mean to you?

[192:32]Does greatness mean that we shrivel up like the libertarians want us to and we don't tell

[192:38]anybody what to do and we sit at the United Nations with our hands folded in our lap and

[192:43]we get lectured by fucking communist third worldists and Russians and the Chinese and

[192:49]Netanyahu?

[192:50]and we raise our placard and wait our turn to speak,

[192:53]that's not what greatness means.

[192:56]Greatness means that we will become

[192:58]the number one superpower ever in history

[193:01]and we will dominate the world.

[193:03]That's what greatness means.

[193:05]That's what it meant to be great as America.

[193:07]When people say make America great again,

[193:10]they don't mean like the 1820s.

[193:13]No, they mean like the 1950s, let's say.

[193:19]when the global empire was handed off

[193:22]from Britain to the United States.

[193:24]That's what people are talking about.

[193:28]When we did great things,

[193:30]that's what I want for America.

[193:32]And the beginning of that,

[193:34]we have to nationalize the population,

[193:37]only those loyal to America.

[193:40]Then we have to nationalize the economy.

[193:42]We need to rebuild our productive capacity.

[193:45]If you can't make tanks, if you can't make ships,

[193:48]If you can't make chips and semiconductors, you cannot project power.

[193:53]So we need to bring the workshops and the factories.

[193:56]We need to bring the manufacturing and advanced manufacturing back to the United States.

[194:02]We need to nationalize our education system.

[194:06]We have to harness the power of our elite human capital forget the best and brightest of the world

[194:11]We need the best and brightest Americans to be plugged in to the research

[194:16]Universities to the Ivy Leagues to get into laboratories get them an R&D budget get them in the military working for the working for the benefit of the country and

[194:25]Then once we shore up the interior then we can project power abroad and then we can make the world in our image

[194:33]And not the image of Barack Obama, where we're backing Malala, Yusufi or whatever, and letting

[194:40]women vote and waving a gay pride flag in Botswana.

[194:44]But then we can actually spread a good message.

[194:49]We can actually spread our influence first, and our ideology second.
